text.text: (Porto-Gonçalves, Carlos Walter, , pp. 39-43).

### Prerequisites

Although this is the first text in the *Philosophy* section, we suggest you study the text on knowledge (<htcite0>) before proceeding.

### Introduction

We will also begin this text with a phrase from Socrates, which gives direction not only to the sciences, but also to the search for understanding the universe in general (<htcite0>; <htcite1>):
• "I only know that I know nothing"
Based on this sentence, let us reflect:

Reflection 1: Going Deeper

How profound is Socrates' conclusion about knowledge?

### Lack of knowledge

If, by chance, upon reading the sentence mentioned by Socrates — which was taken from a broader explanation — you came to the conclusion that you were dealing with an ignorant person, you will be surprised in the following text. If, upon reading this initial sentence of the section, you were surprised, it is possible that you failed to understand the real landscape of education.

#### Socrates' Experiments

Socrates begins his narrative by describing an action taken by a friend of his, Chærephon, who went to the oracle at Delphi to ask if there was any man wiser than Socrates. The prophetess Pythia reportedly answered that there was not (<htcite0>). In Socrates' time, when someone needed to speak with God, it was understood that they required an oracle or prophet, Delphi being one of the most famous and linked to the god Apollo, although it had previously belonged to Gaia (<htcite1>).

Upon receiving the answer, Socrates begins to question himself: “What does the god mean?” and “What is his interpretation?”. Socrates continues by saying that the nature of a god does not allow him to lie. With that, he concluded that the only way to understand the god's answer would be to try to refute it.

Socrates also adds an analysis he made of a man also reputed to be wise, a politician he does not name. Upon speaking with this wise man, he describes trying to help him understand that, despite being considered wise, he actually did not know as much as he imagined. As a consequence, the man came to hate him (<htcite2>).

Reflection 2: How so?

Imagine that, for a long time, you accepted something you were taught, although you never questioned it, to such an extent that you came to place hope in it or treat it as something real.

Would it be easy for you to deny that idea?

Is it easy for people to abandon ideas that sustain their lives or give them hope for the future?

After this experiment, Socrates concluded that he was in a better situation, for the politician knew nothing, despite thinking he knew, while Socrates concluded that “I neither know, nor think that I know” (<htcite0>). Upon repeating the experiment with another wise man, Socrates gained one more enemy.

Socrates was not a very well-liked person in Athens, among other reasons for showing people that they were unaware of many things. Furthermore, he criticized the system of government and, at a certain point, rejected the gods of the city of Athens. As a consequence, he ended up being arrested, tried, and sentenced to death in <htdate0> (<htcite1>; <htcite2>)

Figure 1: Prison of Socrates, Athens, Greece. Photo taken on , during a visit to the archaeological site.
Despite being popularly known as Socrates' prison, due to its proximity to the Athenian Agora, the use of the rooms in the photo is unknown, according to a sign near them.

#### A Question

Baruch Fischhoff, Paul Slovic, and Sarah Lichtenstein published an article in <htdate0> in which they carried out work analogous to what Socrates had done millennia before. In their article, they attempted to measure the following question: “How often are people wrong, yet claim to be correct?” (<htcite0>).

To answer the proposed question, the researchers conducted five experiments, in which they observed that people “are frequently wrong, despite thinking they are correct” (<htcite1>).

Upon analyzing their results, the authors concluded that many of the items presented to people were not stored in their memories. Consequently, they needed to infer an answer. However, people might not have a complete understanding of these inferences (<htcite2>). During the analyses, other works were mentioned that help explain the overconfidence in wrong answers. From these works, we highlight a conclusion by Johnson-Abercrombie (<htcite3>):

source.text: Fischhoff, B., Slovic, P., & Lichtenstein, S.
Fischhoff, B., Slovic, P., & Lichtenstein, S.
Fischhoff, B., Slovic, P., & Lichtenstein, S.
Fischhoff, B., Slovic, P., & Lichtenstein, S.

text.text: • "The[se erroneous] inferences were not arrived at as a series of logical steps but swiftly and almost unconsciously. The validity of the inferences was usually not inquired into; indeed, the process was usually accompanied by a feeling of certainty of being right"

exercise_v2.question: Quando pessoas desconhecem algo, elas tentam puxar de suas memória as respostas, muitas vezes inferindo algo apesar de assumirem ser a resposta correta?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Esta pergunta mostra algo contraditórip, mesmo assim, é o que geralmente ocorre. Se desconhecemos algo, talvez a memória não seja o melhor lugar para obter a resposta, mesmo assim, comumente as pessoas inferem resposta que desconhecem e tendem a acreditar que estão corretas.
